fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Core anchor of the portfolio. Parag Parikh Flexi Cap is a high-conviction, concentrated stock-picker with a global tilt (US tech via a SEBI-regulated wrapper). Beta of 0.54 means it falls far less than the market in bad years — critical for a merchant navy officer with unpredictable income. The fund has a 13-year track record, ₹86,126 Cr AUM, and a 10.3% alpha. It holds global giants like Alphabet and Meta alongside Indian compounders like HDFC Bank and ICICI Bank. This is the ETF-like anchor with the added edge of skilled active management.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: High-conviction mid-cap satellite. Nippon India Growth Mid Cap has delivered 21.6% CAGR over 5 years with a Sharpe of 1.02 — exceptional risk-adjusted returns. The fund manager has been running this portfolio for over 15 years with a proven ability to find compounders before they become giants. Mid-caps are the sweet spot for a 20-year horizon: enough size to be stable, enough room to grow 20x. At ₹8,287 Cr AUM, it is large enough to be liquid but still nimble. This is where the bulk of the wealth creation will happen.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Counterweight to Nippon's mid-cap pick: Motilal Oswal's mid-cap fund has a completely different stock-picking philosophy — a high-conviction, bottom-up portfolio with zero closet indexing. Alpha of 11.24% is among the highest in the category, and the manager has consistently identified compounders before the market catches on. This fund and Nippon together form a powerful mid-cap duo — different teams, different styles, same high-conviction mid-cap goal.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Small-cap satellite — the highest risk, highest reward component of the portfolio. Small-cap stocks in India have historically delivered 18–22% CAGR. Nippon India Small Cap Fund is one of the oldest small-cap funds in India (23 years) with ₹21,153 Cr AUM, meaning it has weathered multiple market cycles. The fund has a 21.2% 5-year CAGR and 32% equity-taxed treatment. For a 20-year horizon, this is non-negotiable — small caps are where 80% of multi-bagger wealth is created in India.

fund.name: Nippon India Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Core large+mid-cap bench — this fund bridges the large-cap stability of India's largest companies with the mid-cap growth runway. Nippon India Large & Mid Cap has a 19.8% 5-year CAGR and a 30-year track record, making it one of India's most battle-tested funds. At a beta of 0.95, it is slightly less volatile than the market, but the alpha of 5.84% ensures it consistently outperforms passive index funds. This is the fund that anchors the mid-cap exposure in the large-cap universe — ideal for Captain Haddock's lump-sum-heavy investment style.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: High-alpha large+mid-cap kicker. ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap has delivered 22.8% CAGR over 3 years and 20.7% over 5 years with a Sharpe of 0.92 — excellent risk-adjusted returns. The fund manager has a 25-year track record and a systematic, bottom-up stock-picking approach. This is the lump-sum allocation that leverages the fund's proven alpha without competing with the SIPs already in motion. The ₹5 lakh lump sum here gives the portfolio an immediate large+mid-cap exposure boost.

plan.setup_phase: Month 1: Deploy the existing ₹20 lakh lump sum across the 5 lump-sum allocations (Parag Parikh Flexi Cap ₹5L, Nippon Mid Cap ₹4L, Motilal Oswal Mid Cap ₹3L, Nippon Small Cap ₹3L, ICICI Large & Mid Cap ₹5L) — total ₹20L deployed immediately. Start all 6 SIPs on the same date. Use your 8 months at sea as a lump-sum deployment window. When back ashore, deploy the next ₹5L as a lump sum across the 5 funds proportionally. Never leave lump sums in a savings account — deploy actively.
review_checkpoints: Q1 (March): Confirm all SIPs are active, lump sums deployed, and total corpus is ₹25–30 lakh. Check if Nippon India Small Cap has crossed 15% — if yes, trim 5% and move to the debt gateway.

Q6 (September): First detailed review. Check if all 3-year CAGR targets are on track. Is Parag Parikh Flexi Cap still at 0.54 beta? Is the small-cap allocation growing at 20%+? Rebalance if any fund has drifted more than 10% from its target allocation.
Year 5: Review portfolio performance against the 5-year benchmark. If Nippon India Small Cap has underperformed the category for 2 straight years, replace it with a different small-cap fund.

Year 10: Major restart. Begin shifting 10–15% of the corpus into a hybrid/conservative allocation as you approach the mid-point of your timeline. Assess daughter's education goal — if the ₹20 lakh/year target is not on track, increase SIPs.

Year 15: Begin systematic withdrawal planning. Start a SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan) from the ELSS-taxed portion to fund the house purchase. Keep education goal funds untouched. Review tax implications of SWP vs. lump sum withdrawal.
Year 20: Final allocation — move 50% of corpus to a conservative hybrid fund. Ensure all tax benefits (ELSS 80C, equity LTCG) are maximised in the final year before exit.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Review your portfolio every quarter. Rebalance if any single fund exceeds its target allocation by 5% or more. For example, if Nippon India Small Cap jumps to 25% from 15%, trim it and redeploy the surplus into Parag Parikh Flexi Cap. Never rebalance during a market crash — that is the worst time to sell. Your job is to stay invested through downturns. If the small-cap allocation falls 30%, do NOT panic-sell. That is a buying opportunity, not a reason to rebalance. Rebalance only when the market is calm and you have a clear view of where the funds have drifted.
plan.rationale: Captain Haddock is a high-earning, aggressive-risk, 20-year horizon investor with an existing ₹20 lakh lump sum and ₹60,000/month investible surplus. The plan is built around 3 core principles: (1) Use lump sums aggressively for the existing corpus to build a strong base, (2) Deploy SIPs to capture compounding over the long run, (3) Keep small-cap and mid-cap exposure high — these are where multi-bagger growth happens. The portfolio is 85% equity, 15% lump sum-heavy, and entirely equity-taxed (no debt component needed under the new regime where your slab is 20% and LTCG is 12.5%). Parag Parikh Flexi Cap anchors the portfolio with global diversification and low beta — critical for a merchant navy officer with unpredictable income cycles. Five actively managed funds across large, mid, and small caps form a powerful satellite structure.
risks: Small-cap funds can fall 40–50% in a bad year — this is normal, not a failure. Do NOT panic-sell. Sit tight and ride the cycle.
Market crashes (like 2008 or 2020) will happen. Your existing lump sum of ₹20 lakh can drop to ₹10–12 lakh in a crash — that is the price of growth. Deploy lump sums gradually, not all at once.

If you fall out of your risk appetite and want to exit mid-way, you can do so at any time without penalty. Do not justify an exit by saying "markets are too high." Markets are always high — that is when you should be invested.
The new tax regime (20% slab) is slightly less tax-efficient for you. However, all equity funds are LTCG-taxed at 12.5% above ₹1.25L — this is still highly efficient for a 20-year horizon.

Gold is intentionally excluded for a 20-year plan. Gold has a poor long-term Sharpe ratio and low alpha vs. the equity funds chosen. Your daughter's education corpus should be in equity funds, not gold.
If your daughter's education goal is 15–18 years away, do NOT touch that corpus. Designate a separate smaller SIP for that goal and keep it in a more conservative fund.

If you are deployed 8 months a year, ensure your SIP is set up before you go to sea — many banks allow SIP mandates to run through salary credits even if you are abroad. Confirm with your fund house that your SIP continues uninterrupted.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase monthly SIP by 10% every year to match assumed salary/earnings growth. From ₹60,000/month today, this becomes ₹66,000/month in Year 2, ₹72,600/month in Year 3, and so on. This single step-up alone can add ₹1–2 crore to your final corpus by Year 20.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Start — 20L lump sum deployed + all SIPs running. 10% in Parag Parikh Flexi Cap as the lump-sum anchor.
Year 5 review. Start building a 5–10% debt buffer for the house purchase. Add a hybrid fund for stability.
Year 10 review. Daughter's education goal is 5 years away — begin shifting education-fund corpus to a lower-volatility hybrid fund. Gold allocation introduced as a hedge.
Year 15 review. Begin systematic withdrawal planning for house purchase. Shift 15% of corpus into a conservative hybrid or arbitrage fund.
Year 20 review. Retirement horizon reached. Gradually shift most corpus to debt and hybrid funds. Start SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan) for monthly income.

plan.perspective: Captain Haddock, you're in the best possible position: high income, 20 years of runway, aggressive risk appetite, and a lump sum ready to be deployed. The plan is designed to maximise wealth creation while respecting your unique lifestyle — you'll invest in lump sums when ashore and let SIPs run when you're at sea. Think of this portfolio as your ship's three masts: Parag Parikh as the steady anchor, Nippon and Motilal as the powerful mid-cap sails, and ICICI and Nippon Small Cap as the wind in the small-cap sails. Over 20 years, this combination should get you to ₹8 crore — your retirement, your daughter's education, and your new home on land.

aps.reasoning: This is an extremely active investment plan. All equity allocations are to actively managed funds with high alpha (10.32%, 12.63%, 11.24%, 6.78%, 5.84%, 9.77%) and explicit stock-picking mandates across flexi cap, mid cap, small cap, and large & mid cap categories. The rationale emphasizes fund manager track records, bottom-up analysis, and high-conviction picks. While the time horizon is long-term (20 years), the quarterly rebalancing guidelines and frequent review schedule indicate active management. Cost minimization is not emphasized - expense ratios range from 1.0-1.2% with no mention of passive alternatives. The portfolio shows genuine active characteristics with high tracking error funds and beta values significantly different from 1.0.

pqs.reasoning: Excellent alignment with Captain Haddock's aggressive risk appetite and 20-year horizon, with specific focus on his merchant navy lifestyle (lump sum + SIP approach). Diversification is good across fund houses and styles but heavily concentrated in equity (85%) with no debt exposure initially, which is appropriate for aggressive profile but lacks asset class diversification. Risk-return profile matches aggressive appetite with realistic 14% equity CAGR projection. Internal consistency has minor issues - allocation schedule shows debt/gold allocations in later years but fund selections are 100% equity funds currently, creating a disconnect. Tax efficiency is excellent - recognizes investor is on new regime, avoids ELSS appropriately, and all funds are equity-taxed. The plan specifically addresses tax implications and leverages equity taxation benefits effectively.
